raw_case:
Rare case of thigh lipoblastoma in an infant: A case report


Introduction and importance

Lipoblastoma is a rare, benign tumor that primarily affects infants and young children, representing a small percentage of soft tissue tumors in this population. Its early diagnosis and surgical management are crucial for favorable outcomes, as lipoblastoma can lead to significant complications if left untreated.

Case presentation

We report the case of an 18-month-old female toddler who presented with a progressively growing, painless mass in the left medial thigh, which had developed over six months. Imaging studies, including CT and MRI, revealed a well-defined, hypodense lobulated mass consistent with lipoblastoma. Surgical excision was performed, and the mass measured approximately 7 by 6 cm. Histopathological examination confirmed the diagnosis of lipoblastoma. The patient experienced a successful recovery without recurrence during a one-year follow-up period.

Discussion

Lipoblastoma typically presents as a painless subcutaneous soft tissue mass that progressively grows. Symptoms often relate to the tumor's location and size or the mass effect on surrounding structures. Imaging studies, particularly CT and MRI, play a crucial role in making a presumptive diagnosis by revealing the tumor's characteristics and components. Total excision remains the treatment of choice for lipoblastoma.

Conclusion

Lipoblastoma should be included in the differential diagnosis of rapidly growing, painless masses in children under three years of age. The treatment of choice is complete surgical resection of the tumor, which is crucial for achieving favorable outcomes and reducing recurrence risk.

Highlights

•Lipoblastoma is a rare benign mesenchymal tumor of the infant and early childhood.•Lipoblastoma should be suspected in case of heterogeneous fatty tumor in children younger than 3 years.•Complete no mutilating resection of the tumor is the treatment of choice.•The prognosis is excellent, with a recurrence rate less than 25% being largely attributed to incomplete resection1

Methods

This case report is conducted following the SCARE criteria for surgical case reports [1].2

Introduction

Lipoblastoma is a rare, benign neoplasm derived from embryonic white fat, primarily affecting infants and young children. They account for approximately 4 % to 6 % of soft-tissue tumors in this population, with a slight male predominance [2,3]. These tumors are characterized by rapid growth and can vary in symptoms based on their location, often causing deformities or compression of adjacent structures. Clinically, lipoblastomas typically present as painless masses, most commonly located in the extremities, although they can also occur in the head and neck region [3].There are two clinical variants: lipoblastoma, which is encapsulated and superficial, accounting for about 70 % of cases, and lipoblastomatosis, which infiltrates surrounding muscle tissue. Both variants share similar histological features [2].Diagnosis is often determined by clinical and radiological findings, particularly through magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). However, definitive diagnosis relies on histological examination, which reveals well-defined lobules of adipocytes, uniform differentiation, and the absence of atypical cells and significant lipoblasts [4,5]. Here, we report a case of a left thigh lipoblastoma in an 18-month-old female toddler, highlighting its clinical presentation, imaging characteristics, and the importance of differential diagnosis in pediatric cases.3

Case presentation

An 18-month-old female child presented with a gradual enlargement of her left medial thigh over the past six months. The mass had been growing painlessly, without any history of trauma or infection. Upon physical examination, a firm, painless, non-pulsatile mass measuring approximately 10 cm was noted in the proximal thigh, fixed to the underlying tissues yet movable relative to the overlying skin. (Fig. 1a & b) There were no signs of inflammation, neurovascular impairment, or inguinal adenopathy.A CT scan revealed a well-defined, hypodense lobulated mass in the left anteromedial compartment of the proximal thigh, extending to the inguinal area. The mass exhibited internal septation with a density comparable to subcutaneous fat, displacing the common and superficial femoral vessels anterolaterally without signs of invasion. The mass also abuts proximal femoral bone but no lytic or sclerotic lesion seen (Fig. 2a & b). An MRI confirmed the presence of a fatty-appearing mass with similar characteristics, showing no encasement of femoral vessels (Fig. 3).The patient underwent surgical excision of the mass through a left anteromedial thigh incision, with careful preservation of the femoral vessels and nerves. The mass measured approximately 7 by 6 cm and weighed 650 g. It was well-encapsulated, soft, and yellowish-white (Fig. 4). Complete resection was achieved, and the surgical wound was closed in layers with a drain placed for 48 h.Histopathological examination revealed a tumor composed of mature fat, myxoid changes, and lipoblasts among adipocytes. The center of the lesion displayed numerous myxoid changes, plexiform capillary channels, and mature adipocyte differentiation (Fig. 5a-d).The patient had an uneventful recovery and was discharged in good condition. At the one-year follow-up, there were no signs of recurrence as shown in the following figure (Fig. 6).4

Discussion

Lipoblastoma is a rare tumor that arises from embryonic white fat, primarily affecting infants and young children. It is characterized histologically by immature adipocytes, defined septa, numerous lipoblasts, and a fine vascular network. Clinically, lipoblastomas typically present as painless subcutaneous soft tissue masses that progressively grow which may mature into lipomas [4]. Symptoms are often related to the lesion's location, size, and mass effect. The extremities and trunk are the most common locations for these tumors, while the head and neck region accounts for 10 % to 15 % of cases [3]. The pathophysiology of lipoblastoma is thought to result from genetic predisposition and alterations in human white fat embryogenesis. Anomalies on chromosome 8 have been linked to the development of lipoblastoma [4].A critical aspect of diagnosing lipoblastoma is differentiating it from liposarcoma, particularly the myxoid type, which is a malignant tumor [6]. Although liposarcoma is uncommon in individuals younger than ten years old, pathologists may find it challenging to distinguish it from lipoblastoma due to histological similarities. Key indicators that suggest liposarcoma include the absence of lobulation, varied growth patterns, and elevated nuclear atypia [7].In addition to liposarcoma, other important differential diagnoses for fat-containing tumors include lipoma, teratoma, dermoid cyst, hibernoma, and involuting hemangioma [2]. True lipomas typically share characteristics with subcutaneous fat, but a fat-rich lipoblastoma can mimic this appearance. The clinical history of progressive growth can help distinguish a fat-rich lipoblastoma from a lipoma. Teratomas and dermoid cysts often contain calcifications or cystic areas in addition to fatty components [8]. Involuting hemangiomas exhibit a characteristic growth pattern marked by rapid proliferation during the first few months of life, followed by stabilization around 9 to 10 months, and a gradual involution phase that continues over several years, ultimately resulting in replacement by fibrofatty tissue by around 7 to 10 years of age [9]. Hibernomas typically occur in individuals in their 30s to 40s and are characterized by unique vascular structures [8].Imaging studies, particularly CT and MRI, play a crucial role in presumptive diagnosis by revealing the tumor's characteristics, origin, composition, and anatomical extent, which aids in surgical planning [10].The prognosis for lipoblastoma is generally excellent, with a recurrence rate of less than 25 %. Most recurrence cases are attributed to incomplete resection of large lesions or muscle infiltration. Recurrence is notably higher in lipoblastomatosis [11].Given the limited literature on this rare condition, we hope this case provides valuable insights and encourages consideration of lipoblastoma in the differential diagnosis for painless masses in young children. In addition to emphasizing the importance of complete surgical resection, we highlight the need for ongoing monitoring for recurrence to ensure the best possible outcomes.

Author contribution

All authors contributed to the conception, writing and editing of the case report. All authors are agreed to be accountable for all aspects of it.

Consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient's parents/legal guardian for publication and any accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Ethical approval

According to our institution, ethical clearance is not required for case reports.

Guarantor

Sileshi Serebe Zeleke.

Funding

Not applicable.

Conflict of interest statement

The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 18-month-old female toddler.
- **Chief Complaint:** Progressive painless mass in the left medial thigh.
- **History of Present Illness:** Gradual enlargement of the left medial thigh mass over six months without pain, trauma, or infection.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Firm, painless, non-pulsatile thigh mass (~10 cm), movable relative to skin, fixed to underlying tissues, no inflammation, neurovascular impairment, or inguinal adenopathy.
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - **CT Scan:** Well-defined, hypodense lobulated mass in the left anteromedial thigh with internal septation, displacing femoral vessels without invasion.  
  - **MRI:** Fatty-looking lobulated mass with no vascular encasement.  
  - **Histopathology:** Tumor composed of mature fat, myxoid changes, lipoblasts, plexiform capillary channels, and mature adipocytes. No atypical cells present.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- The differential diagnosis began with considering fat-containing tumors common in young children (<3 years).
- **Lipoblastoma:** Likely due to the patient's age, a painless progressively growing mass, imaging studies showing lobulated fat-containing mass, and histological findings of benign adipocytic tissue with lipoblasts.
- **Liposarcoma:** Excluded due to the patient’s age (liposarcomas are rare in children under 10), absence of nuclear atypia, and characteristic histology of uniform differentiation and lobulation.
- **Lipoma:** Excluded due to patient age (lipomas are unusual in very young children) and progressive growth of the mass.
- **Teratoma/Dermoid cyst:** Excluded as no calcifications or cystic areas were observed in imaging.
- **Involuting Hemangioma:** Excluded due to the absence of a proliferative phase followed by stabilization/involution, which defines hemangiomas.
- **Hibernoma:** Excluded due to the patient’s young age (hibernomas are more common in adults).

Definitive diagnosis confirmed as lipoblastoma based on imaging and histopathology findings.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Lipoblastoma of the left medial thigh.

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- **Patient Information:** 18-month-old female toddler.
- **Chief Complaint:** Progressive painless mass in the left medial thigh.
- **History of Present Illness:** Gradual enlargement of the left medial thigh mass over six months without pain, trauma, or infection.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Firm, painless, non-pulsatile thigh mass (~10 cm), movable relative to skin, fixed to underlying tissues, no inflammation, neurovascular impairment, or inguinal adenopathy.
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - **CT Scan:** Well-defined, hypodense lobulated mass in the left anteromedial thigh with internal septation, displacing femoral vessels without invasion.  
  - **MRI:** Fatty-looking lobulated mass with no vascular encasement.  
  - **Histopathology:** Tumor composed of mature fat, myxoid changes, lipoblasts, plexiform capillary channels, and mature adipocytes. No atypical cells present.

---

**Format to Follow:**

```
### Resoning:
[Please sort out your thinking process step by step, with each logical step in a separate paragraph.]
<step 1> Specific thinking content of this step
<step 2> Specific thinking content of this step
...
<step n> Specific thinking content of this step

### Answer:
[Just output the diagnostic result without any other explanation.]
```
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient is an 18-month-old female toddler presenting with a progressive, painless mass in the left medial thigh. The age group and location are important, as they guide the differential diagnosis towards pediatric soft tissue tumors, which can be benign or malignant. The painless and gradual nature of the enlargement suggests a lesion that is not acutely inflammatory or rapidly invasive.

<step 2> Physical examination reveals a firm, large (~10 cm), painless, non-pulsatile mass that is mobile relative to the skin but fixed to underlying tissues. The absence of inflammation, neurovascular impairment, and inguinal adenopathy are reassuring signs, suggesting a localized process without significant infection, vascular compromise, or metastatic spread. The fixation to deeper tissues suggests a lesion arising from the muscle or deeper fascial planes rather than superficial subcutaneous tissue.

<step 3> The CT scan describes the mass as well-defined, hypodense, lobulated, with internal septations, displacing femoral vessels without invasion. The hypodensity suggests a component of fluid or fat. The well-defined and lobulated nature, along with displacement rather than invasion of vital structures, are characteristic features often seen in benign or low-grade tumors.

<step 4> The MRI further characterizes the mass as "fatty-looking" and lobulated, with no vascular encasement. The "fatty-looking" appearance on MRI is a strong indicator for lipomatous tumors. The absence of vascular encasement is also a favorable sign, usually indicating a less aggressive lesion.

<step 5> The histopathology report is crucial for definitive diagnosis. It details a tumor composed of mature fat, myxoid changes, lipoblasts, plexiform capillary channels, and mature adipocytes. The absence of atypical cells is a critical finding that rules out malignancy, such as liposarcoma.

<step 6> Synthesizing all the information: the lesion is a large, fatty, lobulated mass in the thigh of a young child. Histologically, it shows features of both mature fat (mature adipocytes) and immature fat (lipoblasts), along with myxoid matrix and specific vascular patterns (plexiform capillary channels). This combination of immature and mature fat cells, myxoid changes, and vascularity, in the absence of cellular atypia, is diagnostic of lipoblastomatosis. Lipoblastomatosis is a benign, congenital tumor of adipose tissue characterized by lobules of lipoblasts (primitive fat cells) often mixed with mature adipocytes, myxoid stroma, and vascular channels. The nodular or diffuse forms can occur, and the lesion described fits the histological profile of lipoblastomatosis.

### Answer:
Lipoblastomatosis

The age group and location are important, as they guide the differential diagnosis towards pediatric soft tissue tumors, which can be benign or malignant. The painless and gradual nature of the enlargement suggests a lesion that is not acutely inflammatory or rapidly invasive.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: pediatric soft tissue tumors,benign pediatric tumors,malignant pediatric tumors,painless thigh mass in toddler,gradual mass enlargement,non-inflammatory lesion
judgment_path.info:
The study assessed the efficacy of ultrasound (US) in diagnosing deep soft-tissue tumors in children, finding that among 82 lesions (25 malignant and 57 benign), no significant differences in age, sex, or symptoms were observed between the two groups. Key independent predictors of malignancy included tumor shape, single or multiple occurrences, and a history of malignancy, with statistical significance for shapes and echogenicity. Although US proved effective for identifying benign lesions such as hemangiomas and adipocytic tumors, the sensitivity of radiologists varied significantly (68% to 96%). The study highlighted challenges in distinguishing between benign and malignant masses based solely on size, borders, or blood supply, indicating that radiologist experience and careful analysis of ambiguous features are crucial for accurate diagnosis.

Soft tissue tumors in children are mostly benign, with origins typically being vascular, fibrous, or neurogenic. The incidence of malignancy in pediatric soft tissue tumors is low, estimated at 11 per million, and is particularly rare in children older than six years without associated conditions like neurofibromatosis. Common signs may include skin findings such as birthmarks or café-au-lait spots. Diagnosis involves clinical examination and imaging techniques, which are crucial for differentiating between various types of tumors and assessing their characteristics.

reasoning_eval.step: Physical examination reveals a firm, large (~10 cm), painless, non-pulsatile mass that is mobile relative to the skin but fixed to underlying tissues. The absence of inflammation, neurovascular impairment, and inguinal adenopathy are reassuring signs, suggesting a localized process without significant infection, vascular compromise, or metastatic spread. The fixation to deeper tissues suggests a lesion arising from the muscle or deeper fascial planes rather than superficial subcutaneous tissue.

judgment_path.info: Lipoblastoma is a rare benign tumor mainly affecting infants and young children, characterized by progressively growing, painless subcutaneous masses, typically found in the extremities. An 18-month-old girl presented with a painless, enlarging mass in the left thigh. Imaging studies, including CT and MRI, revealed a well-defined, hypodense lobulated mass, leading to a diagnosis of lipoblastoma confirmed by histopathology after surgical excision. Early diagnosis and complete surgical resection are crucial as they significantly improve prognosis and minimize recurrence risks, typically less than 25%. Differential diagnosis should consider other fat-containing tumors, including liposarcoma.

reasoning_eval.step: The CT scan describes the mass as well-defined, hypodense, lobulated, with internal septations, displacing femoral vessels without invasion. The hypodensity suggests a component of fluid or fat. The well-defined and lobulated nature, along with displacement rather than invasion of vital structures, are characteristic features often seen in benign or low-grade tumors.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: CT scan, Mass features, Hypodense, Lobulated, Internal septations, Femoral vessels displacement, Tumor invasion, Benign tumors, Low-grade tumors
judgment_path.info: 计算机断层扫描（CT）是一种使用旋转X射线对人体进行分层扫描的成像技术，广泛应用于肿瘤、脑损伤、心血管疾病等的诊断。CT能有效识别肿瘤、脑出血、肺栓塞及肠穿孔等急性病症，并通过提高成像质量，支持早期肺纤维化的诊断。然而，CT扫描存在电离辐射的风险，且过度使用可能导致额外的癌症病例。近年来，技术进步如低剂量CT和AI辅助的诊疗系统正在改善这一点。

reasoning_eval.step: The MRI further characterizes the mass as "fatty-looking" and lobulated, with no vascular encasement. The "fatty-looking" appearance on MRI is a strong indicator for lipomatous tumors. The absence of vascular encasement is also a favorable sign, usually indicating a less aggressive lesion.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: MRI fatty-looking appearance，lipomatous tumors MRI findings，lobulated mass MRI appearance，vascular encasement lesion aggressiveness，MRI lesion characteristics
judgment_path.info: Fat-containing soft-tissue tumors can range from benign lipomas to aggressive liposarcomas, with various imaging characteristics that complicate diagnosis. Symptoms may include palpable masses, often located in superficial or deep tissues. MRI is the primary diagnostic tool, highlighting features such as lesion size, nodularity, and septal thickness to differentiate between benign and malignant forms. Key imaging findings include well-circumscribed, homogeneously fatty masses for lipomas versus irregular, enhancing, and heterogeneous appearances for liposarcomas or atypical lipomatous tumors. Advanced imaging techniques and scoring systems (like Moran's and Nagano's criteria) assist in risk stratification, while histopathological confirmation is essential for lesions that exhibit atypical features. Overall, timely diagnosis and management through multidisciplinary assessment are crucial due to the potential for local aggression and metastasis in malignant cases.

reasoning_eval.step: The histopathology report is crucial for definitive diagnosis. It details a tumor composed of mature fat, myxoid changes, lipoblasts, plexiform capillary channels, and mature adipocytes. The absence of atypical cells is a critical finding that rules out malignancy, such as liposarcoma.

reasoning_eval.step: Synthesizing all the information: the lesion is a large, fatty, lobulated mass in the thigh of a young child. Histologically, it shows features of both mature fat (mature adipocytes) and immature fat (lipoblasts), along with myxoid matrix and specific vascular patterns (plexiform capillary channels). This combination of immature and mature fat cells, myxoid changes, and vascularity, in the absence of cellular atypia, is diagnostic of lipoblastomatosis. Lipoblastomatosis is a benign, congenital tumor of adipose tissue characterized by lobules of lipoblasts (primitive fat cells) often mixed with mature adipocytes, myxoid stroma, and vascular channels. The nodular or diffuse forms can occur, and the lesion described fits the histological profile of lipoblastomatosis.

<Step 2> Lipoblastoma was considered likely due to the patient's age, the painless progressively growing mass, imaging studies showing a lobulated fat-containing mass, and histological findings of benign adipocytic tissue with lipoblasts.
<Step 3> Liposarcoma was excluded because it is rare in children under 10 years old, and the histology showed no nuclear atypia, with characteristic uniform differentiation and lobulation.
<Step 4> Lipoma was excluded due to the patient's young age, as lipomas are unusual in very young children, and the progressive growth of the mass.
<Step 5> Teratoma/Dermoid cyst was excluded as imaging showed no calcifications or cystic areas.
<Step 6> Involuting hemangioma was excluded due to the absence of a proliferative phase followed by stabilization and involution, which defines hemangiomas.
<Step 7> Hibernoma was excluded because hibernomas are more common in adults, and the patient’s young age made this unlikely.
<Step 8> Definitive diagnosis was confirmed as lipoblastoma based on imaging and histopathology findings.
<Step 9> Final diagnosis: Lipoblastoma of the left medial thigh.
